channel.title: NYT > Obituaries
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Bobby Douglas, Pathbreaking Olympic Wrestler, Dies at 83
item.description: In 1964, he was one of the first three African Americans to compete in wrestling at the Games. He went on to have a distinguished coaching career.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Margareta Magnusson,91, Dies; Wrote of Cleaning Up Before Dying
item.description: Her best-selling book encouraged people to tidy up homes and lives as death approached — as a gift for loved ones and to revisit memories.
item.dc:creator: Chris Kornelis
item.category: The Gentle Art of Swedish Death Cleaning: How to Make Your Loved Ones' Lives Easier and Your Own Life More Pleasant (Book)

item.title: Lewis E. Lehrman, Store Chain Heir Who Ran for N.Y. Governor, Dies at 87
item.description: After helping his family’s Rite-Aid drugstore empire flourish, he waged a surprisingly close but losing race as a Reagan Republican against Mario Cuomo in 1982.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.category: Gilder Lehrman Institute of American History
item.media:description: Mr. Lehrman campaigned on Broadway in Lower Manhattan in October 1982. On Election Day, he came close to winning, losing to Mr. Cuomo by 180,000 votes, or two percentage points.

item.title: John F. Burns, Prize-winning Foreign Correspondent for The Times, Dies at 81
item.description: In a 40-year career that brought him two Pulitzers, he reported from trouble spots around the world, eloquently conveying the chaos of war.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.media:credit: Michael Kamber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times international correspondent John F. Burns interviewing people in Baghdad on Dec. 13, 2003, the day the Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein was captured.

item.title: Walid Khalidi, Scholar Called Father of Palestinian Studies, Dies at 100
item.description: As a historian and diplomat, he gave intellectual shape to his people and made sure that they played a role in negotiating their future.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Ernie Anastos, New York’s Everyman Newscaster, Dies at 82
item.description: A television journalist for four decades with 30 Emmy Awards, Mr. Anastos especially enjoyed delivering good news.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:description: Ernie Anastos on the WNYW Fox set in 2010.

item.title: Roscoe Robinson, Gospel Star Who Minted 1960s R&B Hits, Dies at 97
item.description: Although he wasn’t blind, he sang in three different gospel groups known as the Blind Boys before making a splash on the R&B and pop charts.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.category: Five Blind Boys of Mississippi (Music Group)
item.media:description: Roscoe Robinson in an undated photo. The singer Sam Cooke was an early admirer and helped jump-start his career, telling one gospel group, “’Scoe is the guy you need.”

item.title: Yanar Mohammed, 65, Iraqi Women’s Rights Advocate, Is Killed by Gunmen
item.description: She established a network of safe houses for abused women and was an outspoken critic of her country’s repressive institutions, despite the constant threat of violence.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:description: Yanar Mohammed in 2003. She co-founded the Organization of Women’s Freedom in Iraq to protect victims of rape, physical abuse and other forms of ill treatment.

item.title: Overlooked No More: Eleanor Abbott, the Creator of Candy Land
item.description: She invented the game nearly 80 years ago to distract children who were suffering in the hospital during a polio outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Sandra A. Miller and Margaret Muirhead

item.title: Tracing the Sweet Origins of Candy Land and Its Creator
item.description: The writers of an Overlooked obituary about the game’s creator recount how they stumbled upon her and then proceeded to reconstruct her life.
item.dc:creator: Sandra A. Miller and Margaret Muirhead

item.title: Ron Delany, Runner Who Won Olympic Glory for Ireland, Dies at 91
item.description: As a Villanova student, he captured the 1,500-meter event in the 1956 Games in Melbourne. No Irish athlete since then has won another Olympic gold medal in track and field.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:description: Ron Delany crossing the finish line in the 1,500-meter event at the 1956 Olympics in Melbourne, Australia. He set an Olympic record with a time of 3:41.2.

item.title: Bob Harlan, Who Steered the Green Bay Packers to a Revival, Dies at 89
item.description: Under his watch as president, the team brought on key players like Brett Favre, modernized Lambeau Field and once again became a Super Bowl threat.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Bob Harlan in one of the new luxury boxes at Lambeau Field, the Green Bay Packers’ venerable stadium, after its renovation was completed in 2003.

item.title: Stephen Koch, 84, Dies; Champion of a Belatedly Hailed Photographer
item.description: A writer and critic, Mr. Koch struggled for years to shepherd his friend Peter Hujar’s underappreciated, Bohemian-world artwork to posthumous glory.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Peter Schneider Dies at 85; His Novels Explored a Divided Germany
item.description: His best-known work, “The Wall Jumper,” proved prescient in its contention that the country would remain split even after reunification.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Peter Schneider in 1994. He was considered one of the most important literary figures of his generation, in part because his life was so intertwined with the postwar German experience.

item.title: Jan Timman Dies at 74; Fearless Chess Grandmaster and ‘Bon Vivant’
item.description: A Dutchman, he was considered the best player outside the Soviet Union for two decades, although he described himself as “lazy” and was open about using alcohol and drugs early on.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain
item.media:credit: Sepia Times/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Jan Timman in 1982. Unlike most top players of his time, he used a variety of opening strategies, letting his opponents steer the game and fighting them on their own terms, a risky strategy that often succeeded.

item.title: Angelika Saleh, the Angelika of Angelika Film Center, Dies at 90
item.description: After making the journey from prewar Germany to Madison Avenue opulence, she gave her name to one of New York’s most influential indie cinemas.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub

item.title: Anthony J. Leggett Dies at 87; Won Nobel for Theories on Superfluids
item.description: When scientists unwittingly turned helium into a superfluid — a feat many thought was impossible — Dr. Leggett not only recognized what had happened but also explained how.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain

item.title: Dan Simmons, Genre-Leaping Author of ‘The Terror,’ Dies at 77
item.description: He moved easily and prolifically through science fiction, fantasy, horror, thrillers, crime and historical fiction. His book “The Terror” was made into a cable TV series.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Dan Simmons in 1996. “He said from the beginning that he wanted to write what he wanted to write,” his wife, Karen Simmons, said, “and if the publisher didn’t want him to go in that direction, he’d switch publishers.”

item.title: Matt Snell Dies at 84; Carried Jets to Stunning Upset in Super Bowl III
item.description: His pounding runs for the underdog New York team against the Baltimore Colts secured a pivotal win for the American Football League.
item.dc:creator: Jason M. Bailey
item.media:description: Matt Snell of the New York Jets carrying the ball during Super Bowl III in January 1969. Snell was the star of the game, one of the greatest upsets in pro football history.

item.title: Monti Rock III, Gleefully Untalented ‘Tonight Show’ Favorite, Dies at 86
item.description: He couldn’t sing, dance or tell funny stories. But Johnny Carson loved him and his persona: a D-list star clinging to celebrity.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: Ron Galella/Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monti Rock III in 1975. He was a flamboyant dresser, a transparently gay man in the subversive style known as camp in the mid-60s.

item.title: Karen Petrou, Influential Fiscal Policy Analyst and Critic, Dies at 72
item.description: Her insights on financial regulations and monetary policy guided big banks and Washington policymakers.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.media:credit: Sarah Silbiger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Karen Petrou, with a guide dog, Ike, in 2021 at her home in Washington, D.C. She took to using guide dogs in recent decades when her eyesight deteriorated — an alliance she often invoked in her writing.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, Who Revealed Nixon Tapes in Watergate Scandal, Dies at 99
item.description: “There is tape in the Oval Office,” said Mr. Butterfield, a former White House aide, in testimony that rocked the Watergate hearings and led to the president’s resignation.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: Alexander P. Butterfield testifying before the Senate Watergate Committee in July 1973. He revealed to the American public the existence of a secret recording system that President Richard M. Nixon had authorized and that had picked up virtually all of his meetings and telephone conversations.

item.title: John Caldwell, Father of Cross-Country Skiing in U.S., Dies at 97
item.description: A former Olympian himself, he wrote the sport’s bible, coached the American team at five Winter Games and helped make Vermont a hub of Nordic sports.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Bo Gritz, Vietnam Veteran Called a Real-Life Rambo, Dies at 87
item.description: He served in the Special Forces, led a postwar raid to find P.O.W.s and became a voice of the right-wing anti-government fringe.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Bo Gritz in the early 1990s. As a prominent figure among right-wing radicals, he tapped into a rich vein of populist anger over America’s defeat in Vietnam.

item.title: Bernard Lafayette Jr., 85, Dies; Civil Rights Leader Helped Plan Selma March
item.description: A close associate of Martin Luther King Jr. and John Lewis, he was involved in many of the key moments of the Black freedom struggle in the 1960s.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Bernard Lafayette Jr. during a planning session in Montgomery, Ala., in 1961. Dr. Lafayette played a key role in some of the central events of the civil rights movement.

item.title: Colman McCarthy, 87, Journalist Who Waged Peace in the Classroom, Dies
item.description: For decades, he wrote a syndicated column in The Washington Post promoting nonviolence. That became the subject of a course he taught for nearly 40 years.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald
item.media:description: Colman McCarthy in 2002. The newspaper columnist founded the nonprofit Center for Teaching Peace in Washington. “He was a steady and relentless force for good,” the singer Joan Baez said.

item.title: Country Joe McDonald, Whose Antiwar Song Became an Anthem, Dies at 84
item.description: One of the starring acts at Woodstock, he and his band, the Fish, came out of the Bay Area’s psychedelic rock scene. He went on to a long career as a solo artist.
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.category: Country Joe and the Fish (Music Group)
item.media:description: Country Joe McDonald in 1981. The tone of the politics and social commentary in his songs could range from whimsical to snarky.

item.title: Tatjana Wood, Award-Winning Comic Book Colorist, Dies at 99
item.description: She was part of the acclaimed creative teams on comic book series for DC Comics, including Swamp Thing, which she called “Shvampy” in her German accent.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines

item.title: Thaddeus Mosley, Sculptor Who Found Fame in His Last Decade, Dies at 99
item.description: A self-taught artist, he turned reclaimed wood into striking abstract works influenced by Brancusi, Noguchi and African art.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Ross Mantle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thaddeus Mosley at his studio in Pittsburgh in 2023. Of the racial and economic barriers he faced, he said: “I knew that I wasn’t going to get the same recognition, have the same opportunity. But I tried, because it’s something I wanted to do.”
